segments.title: What the Royal Navy expected war games to teach
segments.summary: The 1929 rules connected tabletop decisions to fleet exercises, common doctrine, decentralized command, and readiness for a war that might begin anywhere.
segments.body: The Admiralty's Tactical Division issued a common framework that could be used from individual-ship drills through fleet exercises. Its stated goals included teaching the battle plan, building common tactical doctrine, testing new ideas and material, and encouraging subordinate initiative. Because ships might be assembled from different stations at short notice, shared methods were essential to making an improvised force work together.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces ADM 186/78, its authorship, scope, and tactical training objectives.

segments.title: Cruiser problems disguised the practice of economic war
segments.summary: Cruiser exercises trained scouting, patrol, and commerce-war skills while also rehearsing chart work, communications, command relationships, and war diaries.
segments.body: The politically awkward subject of surface raiding could be taught under the safer label of a cruiser problem. Exercise rules demanded realistic movements, accurate plotting of friendly forces, disciplined command relationships, and written narratives. Clarke links this attention to staff work and reporting with the Royal Navy's effort to avoid a repeat of the communication failures associated with Jutland.

evidence.note: The transcript explains cruiser problems, chart work, command roles, Jutland, and exercise narratives.

segments.title: Turning gunfire and torpedo hits into operational damage
segments.summary: Detailed tables adjusted peacetime firing results for combat and translated hits into lost speed or fighting efficiency rather than a simple sunk-or-survived result.
segments.body: The rules reduced peacetime gunnery performance to represent battle conditions, then applied factors for target size, visibility, smoke, air spotting, and return fire. Torpedo effects varied with warhead and hit location, with damage to rudders, shafts, and propellers capable of slowing an entire formation. This made tactical consequences and fleet maneuver part of damage assessment, not merely the condition of one ship.

evidence.note: The transcript details gunnery factors, torpedo tables, speed reduction, and fighting efficiency.

segments.title: How the rules modeled carrier aircraft operations
segments.summary: The rules modeled serviceability, deck capacity, aircraft range, launch preparation, torpedo attacks, and bombing against submarines in striking operational detail.
segments.body: Aircraft availability declined over sustained operations, while carrier type, lift capacity, engine warm-up, fuel margins, wind, and recovery delays shaped what a force could actually launch. Foreign carriers such as Hosho, Kaga, Lexington, and Saratoga were assigned British comparison types for game purposes. The resulting model supported experiments with torpedo bombers, anti-submarine aviation, and attacks intended to slow an enemy formation for the battle fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript covers submarine visibility, aircraft serviceability, carrier deck operations, attack tables, and radius of action.

segments.title: Communications rules prepared fleets for dispersed warfare
segments.summary: Signal delays and traffic priorities were built into games because carriers and night operations were spreading actions across much wider areas.
segments.body: Wireless messages were assigned realistic transmission times that grew with traffic and priority demands. The Royal Navy was anticipating combat dispersed both globally and tactically, with aviation extending the battlefield and darkness increasing uncertainty. Repeated practice helped officers and staffs exchange information under pressure, even if wartime signals would never be perfect or instantaneous.

evidence.note: The transcript explains wireless timing, traffic priority, dispersed action, and night-fighting complexity.

segments.title: Exercise MZ exposed both air power and carrier vulnerability
segments.summary: Exercise MZ used carriers, air reconnaissance, and torpedo attacks to test fleet tactics while revealing the danger of leaving carriers exposed to enemy cruisers.
segments.body: Air attacks imposed speed losses and gave fleets reconnaissance options that could free cruisers for offensive work. That same freedom created a threat: hostile cruisers could work around a fleet and attack its carriers, while the destroyers nearby were often plane guards rather than a true security screen. The exercise also raised doubts about dispersing torpedo attacks across too many objectives instead of concentrating decisive force.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces MZ, describes air attacks, and draws lessons about carrier protection and attack concentration.

segments.title: Exercise M1 tested concentrated carrier torpedo strikes
segments.summary: Exercise M1 examined carrier speed, early warning, fighter cover, and whether torpedo bombers should attack together or in smaller coordinated groups.
segments.body: The exercise highlighted the need to warn carriers of approaching surface forces and made senior officers responsible for understanding their position and security. Reports also compared torpedo performance and argued that concentrated attacks could be more effective than separate carrier strikes. Clarke notes the practical tension between mass and control, especially at night, when several smaller formations could be easier to coordinate.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses M1, carrier protection, fighter patrols, torpedo results, and strike concentration.

segments.title: Exercise OA showed how submarines could overload a fleet
segments.summary: A fleet battle involving battleships, cruisers, destroyers, aircraft, and submarines showed that submarine pressure could disrupt plans even without scoring the decisive hit.
segments.body: OA tested battle-fleet composition, destroyer placement, scouting, and submarine employment in a complex engagement. K26 passed up a shot at Hood while pursuing the assigned battle-fleet target, illustrating how rigid priorities could waste a major opportunity. The Admiralty concluded that submarines also gained leverage by diverting aircraft and light forces, congesting communications, delaying decisions, and threatening to reduce fleet speed.

evidence.note: The transcript follows OA through its fleet action, K26 episode, damage results, and Admiralty conclusions.

segments.title: Exercise OD rehearsed convoy interception and night battle
segments.summary: An open-ended Mediterranean convoy scenario combined submarines, air reconnaissance, faster heavy ships, and destroyers to explore interception and night fighting.
segments.body: OD set a weaker but faster force against a protected convoy and gave both sides competing operational demands around Malta, Port Said, and Derna. Blue intended to find and shadow the convoy while avoiding a superior force by day, then exploit darkness. The conclusions emphasized continued heavy-ship night training and dependable recognition signals, showing that tactical success depended on identifying friends and coordinating formations after dark.

evidence.note: The transcript lays out OD's forces, convoy scenario, night tactics, and recognition-signal lesson.

segments.title: Exercise OC linked submarine shadowing with air and ASW screens
segments.summary: A submarine reconnaissance exercise prompted experiments with aircraft guidance, destroyer sweeps, ASW screens, and the carrier's position behind the fleet.
segments.body: OC asked submarines to intercept, shadow, and report a fleet so another force could bring it to action. Its results opened questions about using spotter-reconnaissance aircraft with destroyers and ASDIC-equipped vessels, breaking submarine contact at night, and safely positioning a carrier behind the screen. Senior officers cautioned that a carefully arranged exercise could reveal useful problems without providing universally reliable answers.

evidence.note: The transcript describes OC's submarine task, aircraft and destroyer cooperation, ASW screen, and cautions about set-piece results.

segments.title: The 1929 exercises found questions faster than answers
segments.summary: The closing assessment presents the exercises as a learning system that exposed uncertainty and demanded improvement a decade before the Second World War.
segments.body: The Royal Navy was synthesizing lessons from the 1920s while recognizing that aviation, submarines, communications, and night operations had changed the problem inherited from the First World War. Exercises produced more questions and improvement points than grounds for confidence. Clarke's central judgment is that the service benchmarked itself against whichever navy or formation represented the highest capability, then demanded further progress rather than accepting competence as good enough.

evidence.note: The transcript summarizes the exercises as preparation, experimentation, and continual self-comparison.
